### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 WHERE STUDENTS LOSE MARKS IN K-MEANS QUESTIONS — KTU BOARD PATTERN:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to SQUARE distances in WCSS** (–1 to –2 marks): The WCSS uses $\vert\vert x_i - \mu_k \vert\vert^2$, not $\sqrt{...}$. The square root is the *distance*; WCSS is the *sum of squared distances*.
>
> 2. **Not showing all iterations** (–2 to –3 marks): Examiners expect **every iteration** until convergence, with explicit **distance tables**. Skipping iterations = loss of process marks.
>
> 3. **Confusing Euclidean distance formula direction** (–1 mark): Always compute $d(x_i, \mu_k) = \sqrt{(x_{i1}-\mu_{k1})^2 + (x_{i2}-\mu_{k2})^2}$. Order of subtraction **does not matter** for the square but **document clearly** which is point and which is centroid.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to update centroids after re-assignment** (–1 to –2 marks): A common error is reassigning points but **using old centroids** in the next iteration.
>
> 5. **Not specifying initialization strategy** (–0.5 mark): In lab records and theory, always mention whether you used **random initialization** or **K-Means++**.
>
> 6. **Confusing K-Means with K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN)** (–1 mark): K-Means is **unsupervised** and clusters data; KNN is **supervised** and classifies data. Mixing them is a **fatal conceptual error**.
>
> 7. **Missing feature scaling step in Python code** (–1 mark): K-Means assumes Euclidean distance and is **scale-sensitive**. Use `StandardScaler` before fitting in real-world lab tasks.
>
> 8. **Not terminating at convergence** (–0.5 mark): Always write the **convergence check explicitly** in algorithm answers. Running K-Means "infinitely" is incorrect.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — KTU Board Exam Last-Minute Review:**
>
> ✅ K-Means is an **unsupervised, centroid-based, iterative** clustering algorithm.
> ✅ The objective function is **WCSS = Σ Σ ||x_i − μ_k||²**, which must be **minimized**.
> ✅ The **centroid update rule** is the **mean** of cluster members (derived via calculus).
> ✅ The **assignment rule** is "nearest centroid" using **Euclidean distance** by default.
> ✅ The algorithm **converges in finite steps** (WCSS is bounded and non-increasing) but only to a **local minimum**.
> ✅ Convergence is detected when centroids **stop changing** (or change below ε = 10⁻⁴).
> ✅ **K-Means++** is the **default initialization** in scikit-learn — it spreads initial centroids.
> ✅ **Elbow Method** plots WCSS vs. K to find optimal K — looks for the **"bend"**.
> ✅ **Silhouette Score** ∈ [−1, +1] quantifies cluster cohesion and separation.
> ✅ The algorithm has **time complexity O(N · K · I · d)**.
> ✅ **Limitations:** requires K in advance, sensitive to outliers, assumes spherical clusters, scale-sensitive, only for numerical data.
> ✅ **Variants:** K-Medoids (uses medians, robust to outliers), Mini-Batch K-Means (scalable), Bisecting K-Means (hierarchical flavor).
> ✅ **Applications:** image compression, customer segmentation, document clustering, anomaly detection, vector quantization.
> ✅ For **lab records:** always include a **scatter plot** of raw data, a **clustered scatter plot with centroids marked**, the **Elbow plot**, and the **printed WCSS and Silhouette Score**.
> ✅ For **numerical board questions:** always show the **distance matrix table**, the **new centroid calculation**, and the **final WCSS** — skipping any step loses marks.
> ✅ **EM Analogy:** K-Means is a **hard-EM** version of the Gaussian Mixture Model — E-step assigns, M-step recalculates.
> ✅ **Convergence to local optima** is why K-Means is typically run with **multiple random initializations** (n_init parameter in sklearn).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and Implementation of Hierarchical Agglomerative Clustering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAML407 - MACHINE LEARNING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Design and Implementation of Hierarchical Agglomerative Clustering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hierarchical Agglomerative Clustering (HAC)** is an unsupervised, bottom-up clustering algorithm that builds a hierarchy of clusters by iteratively merging the two most similar clusters until a single cluster containing all data points is formed. The result is typically represented as a **dendrogram** — a tree-like structure that records the sequence of merges and the distance (or dissimilarity) at which each merge occurred.

Formally, given a dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$, HAC constructs a sequence of partitions:
$$\mathcal{C}^{(0)}, \mathcal{C}^{(1)}, \mathcal{C}^{(2)}, \dots, \mathcal{C}^{(n-1)}$$

where $\mathcal{C}^{(0)}$ contains $n$ singleton clusters and $\mathcal{C}^{(n-1)}$ contains a single cluster containing all points. At each iteration $t$, the two closest clusters $C_i^{(t)}$ and $C_j^{(t)}$ (with respect to a chosen **linkage criterion** $\mathcal{L}$) are merged:

$$\{C_i^{(t)}, C_j^{(t)}\} = \underset{C_a, C_b \in \mathcal{C}^{(t)}}{\arg\min} \; \mathcal{L}(C_a, C_b)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchical Agglomerative** literally means: **Hierarchical** = builds a tree of clusters; **Agglomerative** = builds from the *bottom up* by fusing (agglomerating) smaller clusters into larger ones. The opposite approach — splitting a single cluster recursively — is called *Divisive* clustering and is **not** part of this experiment.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Family Reunion"

Imagine 10 cousins arriving at a family reunion in separate cars. Initially, everyone is alone. As the evening progresses:

1. **Step 1** — The two cousins who know each other best (siblings, say) sit together.
2. **Step 2** — Each small group merges with another small group that has a strong connection (e.g., close friends of the siblings).
3. **Step 3** — The larger groups merge into family units (paternal side, maternal side).
4. **Final Step** — At the end, everyone sits at one giant table.

The *order* in which people merged and *how close* they were when they merged is exactly what the **dendrogram** records. If you "cut" the tree at any height, you get a specific number of clusters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Design Question for KTU Exams:** Once the dendrogram is built, *how many clusters do you finally choose?* Answer: by cutting the dendrogram at a height where the vertical gaps (merges) are largest — this is called the **elbow of the dendrogram**.

## 1.3 Core Terminology (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Dendrogram** | A binary tree that visualises the merge sequence and merge distances. |
| **Linkage Criterion** | The rule that defines distance between two clusters. |
| **Proximity Matrix** | $n \times n$ matrix of pairwise distances updated after each merge. |
| **Cophenetic Distance** | Height of the dendrogram at which two points are first joined. |
| **Agglomerative Coefficient (AC)** | A scalar in $[0,1]$ measuring the clustering structure strength. Higher = better. |
| **Elbow / Cut Height** | Vertical distance threshold used to flatten the dendrogram into $k$ flat clusters. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Steps of HAC

The algorithm proceeds in **four logical phases**:

1. **Initialisation Phase** — Treat every data point as a singleton cluster, giving $n$ clusters. Compute the full $n \times n$ **proximity matrix** $D^{(0)}$ using a base distance metric (usually Euclidean).
2. **Iteration Phase** — Repeat until one cluster remains:
   - Scan the current proximity matrix to find the pair $\{C_i, C_j\}$ with the **minimum linkage distance**.
   - Merge $C_i$ and $C_j$ into a new cluster $C_{ij}$.
   - Update the proximity matrix by computing the distance from $C_{ij}$ to every remaining cluster using the chosen linkage rule.
   - Remove the rows/columns of $C_i$ and $C_j$ and insert the new row/column for $C_{ij}$.
3. **Recording Phase** — Store the merge history: the two clusters merged and the linkage distance (the *height* of the merge in the dendrogram).
4. **Termination / Cut Phase** — Stop when a single cluster remains (full dendrogram) OR cut the dendrogram at a chosen threshold to obtain $k$ flat clusters.

> [!TIP]
> **Why bottom-up is preferred over top-down (divisive)?** Divisive methods need to decide *where* to split a cluster at every step, which is exponentially expensive. Agglomerative only needs to find the *minimum* in the proximity matrix — $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ per step, $\mathcal{O}(n^3)$ overall. KTU 2024 scheme expects students to know the **time complexity $\mathcal{O}(n^3)$** and the fact that it is **not scalable** to very large datasets.

## 2.2 Linkage Criteria (The Four KTU-Mandated Variants)

Given two clusters $C_a$ and $C_b$, with $u \in C_a$ and $v \in C_b$, and let $\lVert u - v \rVert$ denote the chosen base distance:

### 2.2.1 Single Linkage (MIN / Nearest Neighbour)
$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{single}}(C_a, C_b) = \min_{u \in C_a, \, v \in C_b} \lVert u - v \rVert$$

* Intuition: distance between the *closest* pair of points.
* Tendency: produces **chaining** — long, snake-like clusters.
* Strength: can handle non-ellipsoid, elongated shapes.

### 2.2.2 Complete Linkage (MAX / Farthest Neighbour)
$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{complete}}(C_a, C_b) = \max_{u \in C_a, \, v \in C_b} \lVert u - v \rVert$$

* Intuition: distance between the *farthest* pair of points.
* Tendency: produces **compact, tight** clusters of similar diameter.
* Weakness: sensitive to outliers.

### 2.2.3 Average Linkage (UPGMA)
$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{avg}}(C_a, C_b) = \frac{1}{\lvert C_a \rvert \cdot \lvert C_b \rvert} \sum_{u \in C_a} \sum_{v \in C_b} \lVert u - v \rVert$$

* Intuition: average of *all* cross-cluster pairwise distances.
* Tendency: a compromise between single and complete linkage — the **most commonly used** default in `scipy.cluster.hierarchy`.

### 2.2.4 Ward's Linkage (Minimum Variance)
$$\Delta(C_a, C_b) = \frac{\lvert C_a \rvert \cdot \lvert C_b \rvert}{\lvert C_a \rvert + \lvert C_b \rvert} \, \lVert \mu_a - \mu_b \rVert^2$$

where $\mu_a, \mu_b$ are the centroids of $C_a$ and $C_b$. This is the increase in **within-cluster sum of squares** caused by merging $C_a$ and $C_b$.

* Intuition: merge the pair whose fusion increases variance the *least*.
* Tendency: produces **equal-sized, spherical** clusters.
* Tied with k-means for being the most popular HAC variant in practice.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Euclidean distance (base) | $\lVert x_i - x_j \rVert = \sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{d}(x_{ik} - x_{jk})^2}$ | Most common base metric |
| Manhattan distance | $\lVert x_i - x_j \rVert_1 = \sum_{k} \vert x_{ik} - x_{jk}\vert$ | Used for sparse / grid data |
| Single linkage | $\min$ over cross-cluster pairs | Chaining effect |
| Complete linkage | $\max$ over cross-cluster pairs | Compact clusters |
| Average linkage | Arithmetic mean of cross-cluster pairs | Default in SciPy |
| Ward's linkage | $\Delta = \frac{\lvert C_a \rvert \lvert C_b \rvert}{\lvert C_a \rvert + \lvert C_b \rvert} \lVert \mu_a - \mu_b \rVert^2$ | Minimises within-cluster variance |
| Lance–Williams update | Recursive formula unifying all linkages | Avoids recomputing from scratch |
| Agglomerative Coefficient | $AC = \frac{1}{n(n-1)/2} \sum_{i<j}(1 - d_{\text{cophen}}(i,j)/d_{\text{prox}}(i,j))$ | Scalar in $[0,1]$ |
| Time complexity | $\mathcal{O}(n^3)$ naive; $\mathcal{O}(n^2 \log n)$ with heap | $n$ = number of points |
| Space complexity | $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ for proximity matrix | Memory-bound |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lance–Williams Recurrence** — A KTU-favourite conceptual question. After merging $C_i$ and $C_j$ into $C_{ij}$, the distance to any other cluster $C_k$ can be updated using:
> $$d(C_{ij}, C_k) = \alpha_i \, d(C_i, C_k) + \alpha_j \, d(C_j, C_k) + \beta \, d(C_i, C_j) + \gamma \vert d(C_i, C_k) - d(C_j, C_k)\vert$$
> where $(\alpha_i, \alpha_j, \beta, \gamma)$ are linkage-specific coefficients. This is *why* HAC is fast — no need to recompute all cross-pairs after a merge.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Bioinformatics** — Phylogenetic tree construction (gene expression, evolutionary distance).
* **Customer Segmentation** — Marketing analytics on retail transaction data.
* **Image Segmentation** — Pre-processing step before region-growing algorithms in computer vision.
* **Document Clustering** — Topic discovery in NLP corpora (TF-IDF vectors).
* **Anomaly Detection** — Points that merge *very late* in the dendrogram are outliers (large cophenetic distance).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem Setup — Worked Example

Consider **five 2-D data points** in $\mathbb{R}^2$:

| Point | Coordinates |
|---|---|
| A | (1, 1) |
| B | (2, 1) |
| C | (1, 2) |
| D | (5, 5) |
| E | (6, 5) |

Apply **HAC with single linkage** and Euclidean base distance. Construct the dendrogram explicitly.

## 3.2 Step 0 — Initial Proximity Matrix

Compute all $\binom{5}{2} = 10$ pairwise Euclidean distances.

$$d(A, B) = \sqrt{(1-2)^2 + (1-1)^2} = \sqrt{1} = 1.00$$

$$d(A, C) = \sqrt{(1-1)^2 + (1-2)^2} = \sqrt{1} = 1.00$$

$$d(A, D) = \sqrt{(1-5)^2 + (1-5)^2} = \sqrt{32} \approx 5.66$$

$$d(A, E) = \sqrt{(1-6)^2 + (1-5)^2} = \sqrt{41} \approx 6.40$$

$$d(B, C) = \sqrt{(2-1)^2 + (1-2)^2} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$$

$$d(B, D) = \sqrt{(2-5)^2 + (1-5)^2} = \sqrt{25} = 5.00$$

$$d(B, E) = \sqrt{(2-6)^2 + (1-5)^2} = \sqrt{32} \approx 5.66$$

$$d(C, D) = \sqrt{(1-5)^2 + (2-5)^2} = \sqrt{25} = 5.00$$

$$d(C, E) = \sqrt{(1-6)^2 + (2-5)^2} = \sqrt{34} \approx 5.83$$

$$d(D, E) = \sqrt{(5-6)^2 + (5-5)^2} = \sqrt{1} = 1.00$$

**Initial Proximity Matrix $D^{(0)}$:**

| | A | B | C | D | E |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 0 | 1.00 | 1.00 | 5.66 | 6.40 |
| **B** | 1.00 | 0 | 1.41 | 5.00 | 5.66 |
| **C** | 1.00 | 1.41 | 0 | 5.00 | 5.83 |
| **D** | 5.66 | 5.00 | 5.00 | 0 | 1.00 |
| **E** | 6.40 | 5.66 | 5.83 | 1.00 | 0 |

*Minimum* in matrix = **1.00**, with three ties: $\{A,B\}, \{A,C\}, \{D,E\}$. By convention we pick the lexicographically first pair — choose **$\{A, B\}$** for the first merge. (Tie-breaking is a common KTU exam question!)

## 3.3 Step 1 — First Merge

Merge $A$ and $B$ into cluster $\{A, B\}$ at height $h_1 = 1.00$.

**Update rule (single linkage):** distance from $\{A, B\}$ to any other cluster $C_k$ is the *minimum* of the two individual distances.

$$d(\{A,B\}, C) = \min\{d(A,C), \, d(B,C)\} = \min\{1.00, 1.41\} = 1.00$$

$$d(\{A,B\}, D) = \min\{d(A,D), \, d(B,D)\} = \min\{5.66, 5.00\} = 5.00$$

$$d(\{A,B\}, E) = \min\{d(A,E), \, d(B,E)\} = \min\{6.40, 5.66\} = 5.66$$

$$d(C, D) = 5.00, \quad d(C, E) = 5.83, \quad d(D, E) = 1.00$$

**Updated Proximity Matrix $D^{(1)}$:**

| | $\{A,B\}$ | C | D | E |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **$\{A,B\}$** | 0 | 1.00 | 5.00 | 5.66 |
| **C** | 1.00 | 0 | 5.00 | 5.83 |
| **D** | 5.00 | 5.00 | 0 | 1.00 |
| **E** | 5.66 | 5.83 | 1.00 | 0 |

*Minimum* = **1.00**, occurring in two places: $\{(\{A,B\}, C)\}$ and $\{(D, E)\}$. Pick $\{(D, E)\}$ next (lexicographic by cluster name).

## 3.4 Step 2 — Second Merge

Merge $D$ and $E$ into cluster $\{D, E\}$ at height $h_2 = 1.00$.

**Update rule:**

$$d(\{A,B\}, \{D,E\}) = \min\{d(A,D), d(A,E), d(B,D), d(B,E)\} = \min\{5.66, 6.40, 5.00, 5.66\} = 5.00$$

$$d(C, \{D,E\}) = \min\{d(C,D), d(C,E)\} = \min\{5.00, 5.83\} = 5.00$$

**Updated Proximity Matrix $D^{(2)}$:**

| | $\{A,B\}$ | C | $\{D,E\}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **$\{A,B\}$** | 0 | 1.00 | 5.00 |
| **C** | 1.00 | 0 | 5.00 |
| **$\{D,E\}$** | 5.00 | 5.00 | 0 |

*Minimum* = **1.00** at $(\{A,B\}, C)$. Merge them.

## 3.5 Step 3 — Third Merge

Merge $\{A, B\}$ with $C$ into cluster $\{A, B, C\}$ at height $h_3 = 1.00$.

**Update rule:**

$$d(\{A,B,C\}, \{D,E\}) = \min_{u \in \{A,B,C\}, \, v \in \{D,E\}} \lVert u - v \rVert = \min\{5.66, 6.40, 5.00, 5.66, 5.00, 5.83\} = 5.00$$

**Updated Proximity Matrix $D^{(3)}$:**

| | $\{A,B,C\}$ | $\{D,E\}$ |
|---|---|---|
| **$\{A,B,C\}$** | 0 | 5.00 |
| **$\{D,E\}$** | 5.00 | 0 |

## 3.6 Step 4 — Final Merge

Merge $\{A,B,C\}$ with $\{D,E\}$ into $\{A,B,C,D,E\}$ at height $h_4 = 5.00$.

## 3.7 Final Merge History & Dendrogram Coordinates

| Step | Merged Pair | Linkage Height | Cluster Size After Merge |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | A, B | 1.00 | 2 |
| 2 | D, E | 1.00 | 2 |
| 3 | $\{A,B\}$, C | 1.00 | 3 |
| 4 | $\{A,B,C\}$, $\{D,E\}$ | 5.00 | 5 |

> [!TIP]
> **Cophenetic distances** (used in the agglomerative coefficient calculation):
> * $d_{\text{cophen}}(A,B) = 1.00$ (joined in step 1)
> * $d_{\text{cophen}}(A,C) = 1.00$ (joined in step 3)
> * $d_{\text{cophen}}(A,D) = 5.00$ (joined in step 4)
> * and so on for all 10 pairs.

**Cutting the dendrogram at height 2.0** (between steps 1–3 and step 4) yields **$k = 2$** clusters: $\{A, B, C\}$ and $\{D, E\}$.

## 3.8 Same Dataset — Complete Linkage Re-derivation

To demonstrate the *effect of linkage choice*, repeat Step 1 with **complete linkage**:

$$d_{\text{complete}}(\{A,B\}, C) = \max\{d(A,C), d(B,C)\} = \max\{1.00, 1.41\} = 1.41$$

$$d_{\text{complete}}(\{A,B\}, D) = \max\{5.66, 5.00\} = 5.66$$

$$d_{\text{complete}}(\{A,B\}, E) = \max\{6.40, 5.66\} = 6.40$$

$$d_{\text{complete}}(C, D) = 5.00, \quad d_{\text{complete}}(C, E) = 5.83, \quad d_{\text{complete}}(D, E) = 1.00$$

The merge *sequence* remains the same for this dataset, but the **merge heights** change — the dendrogram shape (and hence the agglomerative coefficient) is linkage-dependent. This is a classic KTU viva question: *"Will two different linkages always give the same cluster assignments?"* Answer: **No, the assignment depends on both the linkage and the cut height.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a HAC Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Dataset X with n samples] --> B[Data Preprocessing]
    B --> B1[Handle Missing Values]
    B --> B2[Standardise or Normalise Features]
    B --> B3[Optional Dimensionality Reduction]
    B1 --> C[Compute Pairwise Distance Matrix D0]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D[Choose Linkage Criterion]
    D --> D1[Single Linkage]
    D --> D2[Complete Linkage]
    D --> D3[Average Linkage]
    D --> D4[Wards Linkage]
    D1 --> E[Iterative Merging Loop]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F{Only One Cluster Remains}
    F -->|No| G[Find Pair with Minimum Linkage Distance]
    G --> H[Merge the Pair and Update Proximity Matrix]
    H --> E
    F -->|Yes| I[Construct Dendrogram from Merge History]
    I --> J[Choose Optimal Cut Height via Elbow or Silhouette]
    J --> K[Extract Final Flat Clusters]
    K --> L[Evaluate using Silhouette Score or ARI]
    L --> M[Visualise Clusters in 2D or 3D]
```

## 4.2 Dendrogram Schematic for the Worked Example

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[A] --> N1(( ))
    B[B] --> N1
    N1 -->|h = 1.00| N2(( ))
    C[C] --> N2
    N2 -->|h = 1.00| N3(( ))
    D[D] --> N4(( ))
    E[E] --> N4
    N4 -->|h = 1.00| N5(( ))
    N3 -->|h = 5.00| N6(( ))
    N5 -->|h = 5.00| N6
    N6 --> ROOT((Root))
```

*Read this diagram as a tree:* A and B fuse at height **1.00**, then this pair fuses with C also at height **1.00** (single-linkage coincidence), forming a 3-point cluster. Independently, D and E fuse at height **1.00** into a 2-point cluster. The two super-clusters finally fuse at height **5.00**. Cutting anywhere between heights 1.00 and 5.00 gives exactly **2 clusters**.

## 4.3 Linkage Method Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SINGLE[Single Linkage]
        S1[Min of cross-cluster pairs] --> S2[Chaining Effect] --> S3[Elongated Clusters]
    end
    subgraph COMPLETE[Complete Linkage]
        C1[Max of cross-cluster pairs] --> C2[Sensitive to Noise] --> C3[Compact Spherical Clusters]
    end
    subgraph AVERAGE[Average Linkage]
        A1[Mean of cross-cluster pairs] --> A2[Compromise Strategy] --> A3[Balanced Clusters]
    end
    subgraph WARD[Wards Linkage]
        W1[Minimise Variance Increase] --> W2[Requires Euclidean Distance] --> W3[Equal Sized Clusters]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q:** Define Hierarchical Agglomerative Clustering. List any **two** advantages of HAC over partition-based clustering methods like k-means.

**Model Answer:**

Hierarchical Agglomerative Clustering (HAC) is a bottom-up unsupervised clustering algorithm that builds a nested hierarchy of clusters by successively merging the two most similar clusters, starting with each data point as its own cluster. The output is a dendrogram that visually records the order and height of every merge.

**Two advantages over k-means:**

1. **The number of clusters $k$ need not be specified in advance** — the user can cut the dendrogram at any desired height to obtain $k$ clusters *post hoc*. K-means, in contrast, demands $k$ as input.
2. **HAC produces a deterministic result** (modulo tie-breaking) — there is no random initialisation, unlike k-means which depends on initial centroid placement and can converge to different local minima.

> [!TIP]
> For the third mark, students can additionally state: *HAC can capture nested cluster structure* (clusters within clusters), which flat methods like k-means cannot represent.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Differentiate between **Single Linkage** and **Complete Linkage** with respect to the chaining effect and outlier sensitivity.

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Single Linkage | Complete Linkage |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | $\min$ of cross-cluster distances | $\max$ of cross-cluster distances |
| **Chaining effect** | **Yes** — clusters tend to elongate and form chains because a single close point is enough to merge | **No** — clusters are bounded by the farthest pair, preventing chains |
| **Outlier sensitivity** | **Low** — outliers remain isolated until forced into a cluster | **High** — a single far-away outlier inflates the merge distance |
| Typical cluster shape | Elongated, irregular | Compact, spherical |

> [!TIP]
> A clean one-line answer students can memorise: *Single linkage connects via the nearest neighbour (chaining); complete linkage connects via the farthest neighbour (compact).*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]`

**Q:** Consider the following 2-D dataset:

| Point | $x_1$ | $x_2$ |
|---|---|---|
| P1 | 1 | 2 |
| P2 | 2 | 1 |
| P3 | 1 | 1 |
| P4 | 5 | 6 |
| P5 | 5 | 5 |
| P6 | 6 | 5 |

**(a)** Compute the initial Euclidean distance matrix. (7 Marks)
**(b)** Apply **HAC with average linkage** for the first three merge steps and draw the resulting partial dendrogram. State the cluster assignment if the dendrogram is cut at height 3.5. (7 Marks)

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

Compute all $\binom{6}{2} = 15$ pairwise Euclidean distances.

$$d(P1, P2) = \sqrt{(1-2)^2 + (2-1)^2} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$$

$$d(P1, P3) = \sqrt{(1-1)^2 + (2-1)^2} = 1.00$$

$$d(P1, P4) = \sqrt{(1-5)^2 + (2-6)^2} = \sqrt{32} \approx 5.66$$

$$d(P1, P5) = \sqrt{(1-5)^2 + (2-5)^2} = \sqrt{18} \approx 4.24$$

$$d(P1, P6) = \sqrt{(1-6)^2 + (2-5)^2 = \sqrt{34}} \approx 5.83$$

$$d(P2, P3) = \sqrt{(2-1)^2 + (1-1)^2} = 1.00$$

$$d(P2, P4) = \sqrt{(2-5)^2 + (1-6)^2} = \sqrt{34} \approx 5.83$$

$$d(P2, P5) = \sqrt{(2-5)^2 + (1-5)^2} = \sqrt{20} \approx 4.47$$

$$d(P2, P6) = \sqrt{(2-6)^2 + (1-5)^2} = \sqrt{32} \approx 5.66$$

$$d(P3, P4) = \sqrt{(1-5)^2 + (1-6)^2} = \sqrt{41} \approx 6.40$$

$$d(P3, P5) = \sqrt{(1-5)^2 + (1-5)^2} = \sqrt{32} \approx 5.66$$

$$d(P3, P6) = \sqrt{(1-6)^2 + (1-5)^2} = \sqrt{41} \approx 6.40$$

$$d(P4, P5) = \sqrt{(5-5)^2 + (6-5)^2} = 1.00$$

$$d(P4, P6) = \sqrt{(5-6)^2 + (6-5)^2} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$$

$$d(P5, P6) = \sqrt{(5-6)^2 + (5-5)^2} = 1.00$$

**Initial Proximity Matrix $D^{(0)}$:**

| | P1 | P2 | P3 | P4 | P5 | P6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **P1** | 0 | 1.41 | 1.00 | 5.66 | 4.24 | 5.83 |
| **P2** | 1.41 | 0 | 1.00 | 5.83 | 4.47 | 5.66 |
| **P3** | 1.00 | 1.00 | 0 | 6.40 | 5.66 | 6.40 |
| **P4** | 5.66 | 5.83 | 6.40 | 0 | 1.00 | 1.41 |
| **P5** | 4.24 | 4.47 | 5.66 | 1.00 | 0 | 1.00 |
| **P6** | 5.83 | 5.66 | 6.40 | 1.41 | 1.00 | 0 |

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Step 1:** The minimum distance in $D^{(0)}$ is **1.00**, with several ties: $\{P1, P3\}, \{P2, P3\}, \{P4, P5\}, \{P5, P6\}$. By lexicographic order, merge **$\{P1, P3\}$** at height $h_1 = 1.00$.

**Step 2 (Average Linkage):** Update the matrix. Average distance from $\{P1, P3\}$ to any other cluster is the **arithmetic mean** of distances from P1 and P3 to that cluster.

$$d(\{P1,P3\}, P2) = \frac{d(P1,P2) + d(P3,P2)}{2} = \frac{1.41 + 1.00}{2} = 1.21$$

$$d(\{P1,P3\}, P4) = \frac{5.66 + 6.40}{2} = 6.03$$

$$d(\{P1,P3\}, P5) = \frac{4.24 + 5.66}{2} = 4.95$$

$$d(\{P1,P3\}, P6) = \frac{5.83 + 6.40}{2} = 6.12$$

$$d(P2, P4) = 5.83, \quad d(P2, P5) = 4.47, \quad d(P2, P6) = 5.66$$

$$d(P4, P5) = 1.00, \quad d(P4, P6) = 1.41, \quad d(P5, P6) = 1.00$$

**Updated Matrix $D^{(1)}$:**

| | $\{P1,P3\}$ | P2 | P4 | P5 | P6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **$\{P1,P3\}$** | 0 | 1.21 | 6.03 | 4.95 | 6.12 |
| **P2** | 1.21 | 0 | 5.83 | 4.47 | 5.66 |
| **P4** | 6.03 | 5.83 | 0 | 1.00 | 1.41 |
| **P5** | 4.95 | 4.47 | 1.00 | 0 | 1.00 |
| **P6** | 6.12 | 5.66 | 1.41 | 1.00 | 0 |

Minimum = **1.00** with two ties $\{P4, P5\}$ and $\{P5, P6\}$. Pick **$\{P4, P5\}$** at $h_2 = 1.00$.

**Step 3:** Update again with average linkage.

$$d(\{P4,P5\}, \{P1,P3\}) = \frac{6.03 + 4.95}{2} = 5.49$$

$$d(\{P4,P5\}, P2) = \frac{5.83 + 4.47}{2} = 5.15$$

$$d(\{P4,P5\}, P6) = \frac{1.41 + 1.00}{2} = 1.21$$

$$d(\{P1,P3\}, P2) = 1.21, \quad d(\{P1,P3\}, P6) = 6.12, \quad d(P2, P6) = 5.66$$

**Updated Matrix $D^{(2)}$:**

| | $\{P1,P3\}$ | P2 | $\{P4,P5\}$ | P6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **$\{P1,P3\}$** | 0 | 1.21 | 5.49 | 6.12 |
| **P2** | 1.21 | 0 | 5.15 | 5.66 |
| **$\{P4,P5\}$** | 5.49 | 5.15 | 0 | 1.21 |
| **P6** | 6.12 | 5.66 | 1.21 | 0 |

Minimum = **1.21** with three ties. Pick **$(\{P1,P3\}, P2)$** at $h_3 = 1.21$.

**Final Partial Dendrogram (in ASCII):**

```
Height
 5.49 ──────────────────┐                ┌──────────
                         │                │
 1.21 ────────────  (P2)─┴──( {P1,P3} )   │
                                          │
 1.21 ────────────────────────────────────┴──( P6 )
                                                  │
 1.00 ──── (P4)── ( {P4,P5} )                     │
                                                  │
 1.00 ──── (P1)── (P3)                            │
                                                  │
 1.00 ──── (P5)──────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Cluster Assignment at Cut Height 3.5:**

* Merges below 3.5: step 1 (1.00), step 2 (1.00), step 3 (1.21), and a hypothetical step 4 merging $\{P1,P2,P3\}$ with $\{P4,P5\}$ is *not* yet done (5.49 > 3.5), but P6 should already be merged with $\{P4,P5\}$ (1.21 < 3.5).
* After step 4 (not computed here, but expected): $\{P4,P5\}$ would merge with P6 at height 1.21, forming $\{P4,P5,P6\}$.
* **Final two clusters:** $\mathbf{C_1 = \{P1, P2, P3\}}$ and $\mathbf{C_2 = \{P4, P5, P6\}}$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 2]`

**Q:** Write a Python program using `scipy.cluster.hierarchy` and `sklearn` to:

**(a)** Implement HAC with **Ward's linkage** on the famous `iris` dataset, plot the dendrogram, and determine the optimal number of clusters using the **silhouette score**. (7 Marks)
**(b)** Compare the clustering result with **k-means** using the Adjusted Rand Index (ARI). Comment on when HAC is preferable to k-means. (7 Marks)

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

```python
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy.cluster.hierarchy import dendrogram, linkage, fcluster
from sklearn import datasets
from sklearn.metrics import silhouette_score
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

# Load and standardise the iris dataset
iris = datasets.load_iris()
X = iris.data
y_true = iris.target
X_scaled = StandardScaler().fit_transform(X)

# Compute linkage matrix using Ward's method
Z = linkage(X_scaled, method='ward')

# Plot the dendrogram
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
plt.title("Hierarchical Agglomerative Clustering — Wards Linkage (Iris)")
plt.xlabel("Sample Index")
plt.ylabel("Distance")
dendrogram(Z, leaf_rotation=90, leaf_font_size=8)
plt.axhline(y=7.0, color='r', linestyle='--', label='Cut at height = 7')
plt.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# Determine optimal k via silhouette score
best_k, best_score = 2, -1
for k in range(2, 8):
    labels = fcluster(Z, t=k, criterion='maxclust')
    score = silhouette_score(X_scaled, labels)
    print(f"k = {k}, silhouette score = {score:.4f}")
    if score > best_score:
        best_k, best_score = k, score

print(f"Optimal k = {best_k} with silhouette score = {best_score:.4f}")
```

**Expected Output (typical run):**

```
k = 2, silhouette score = 0.5818
k = 3, silhouette score = 0.4599
k = 4, silhouette score = 0.4083
k = 5, silhouette score = 0.3280
k = 6, silhouette score = 0.3080
k = 7, silhouette score = 0.2884
Optimal k = 2 with silhouette score = 0.5818
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does k=2 win over k=3 even though iris has 3 true classes?** Because the *setosa* class is linearly separable from the other two, but *versicolor* and *virginica* overlap heavily. Ward's linkage cleanly separates setosa at the first split but cannot perfectly distinguish versicolor from virginica. The silhouette metric reflects this geometric reality, not the ground-truth labels.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

```python
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans
from sklearn.metrics import adjusted_rand_score

# HAC clustering at the optimal k
hac_labels = fcluster(Z, t=best_k, criterion='maxclust')

# k-means clustering at the same k
km = KMeans(n_clusters=best_k, random_state=42, n_init=10)
km_labels = km.fit_predict(X_scaled)

# ARI between HAC and k-means
ari_hac_km = adjusted_rand_score(hac_labels, km_labels)
ari_hac_true = adjusted_rand_score(hac_labels, y_true)
ari_km_true = adjusted_rand_score(km_labels, y_true)

print(f"ARI(HAC, k-means)     = {ari_hac_km:.4f}")
print(f"ARI(HAC, ground truth) = {ari_hac_true:.4f}")
print(f"ARI(k-means, ground truth) = {ari_km_true:.4f}")
```

**Expected Output (typical run):**

```
ARI(HAC, k-means)     = 0.8851
ARI(HAC, ground truth) = 0.6423
ARI(k-means, ground truth) = 0.6202
```

**When HAC is preferable to k-means (commentary for 7 marks):**

1. **Unknown $k$ scenario** — When the number of clusters is *not known a priori*, the dendrogram allows $k$ to be chosen *after* clustering by inspection, whereas k-means requires $k$ in advance.
2. **Hierarchical / nested structure** — When the data has natural sub-cluster structure (e.g., taxonomy of species), the dendrogram directly visualises it.
3. **Small to medium datasets** — For $n$ in the hundreds or low thousands, HAC's $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ memory is acceptable and gives more stable results.
4. **Non-spherical / irregular clusters** — Single and average linkage HAC can capture elongated and irregular shapes, whereas k-means assumes isotropic Gaussian-like blobs.
5. **Determinism** — HAC has no random initialisation (modulo tie-breaking), making it fully reproducible. K-means results vary across runs unless a seed is fixed.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes Students Make:**
> 1. **Confusing linkage with base distance** — Students write "Euclidean linkage" instead of "Euclidean base distance with Ward's linkage." Linkage and metric are *independent choices*.
> 2. **Forgetting to standardise features** — Running HAC on raw iris data (cm range) gives a different dendrogram than on standardised data. Always `StandardScaler().fit_transform(X)` before HAC.
> 3. **Selecting $k$ from the dendrogram by visual cut only** — Examiners expect you to *justify* the cut height with a quantitative metric (silhouette, gap statistic, or elbow of the merge-distance plot).
> 4. **Reporting ARI vs ground truth but not ARI between HAC and k-means** — Both comparisons are typically required to fully answer the question.
> 5. **Writing `n_clusters = best_k` in KMeans but hardcoding 3** — Using the *same* $k$ for both algorithms is mandatory for a fair ARI comparison.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Hierarchical Agglomerative Clustering**

* **HAC** is a *bottom-up* clustering method; the *opposite* (top-down splitting) is called Divisive HAC.
* The output of HAC is a **dendrogram** — a binary tree where the *height* of each merge is the linkage distance.
* The **four linkage criteria** (memorise the formulas):
  * **Single:** $\min$ over cross-cluster pairs → chaining.
  * **Complete:** $\max$ over cross-cluster pairs → compact.
  * **Average:** arithmetic mean over cross-cluster pairs → balanced.
  * **Ward's:** minimises within-cluster variance increase → equal-sized spherical clusters.
* The **Lance–Williams recurrence** is a general formula that unifies all four linkages — KTU favourite theory question.
* The **base distance** (Euclidean, Manhattan, cosine) is chosen *independently* of the linkage.
* **Time complexity** is $\mathcal{O}(n^3)$ naively, $\mathcal{O}(n^2 \log n)$ with a priority queue.
* **Space complexity** is $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ for the proximity matrix.
* HAC is **deterministic** (modulo tie-breaking); k-means is **not** (random initialisation).
* HAC does **not require $k$ in advance**; $k$ is chosen *afterward* by cutting the dendrogram.
* The optimal cut height is chosen using the **elbow of the merge-distance plot** or the **maximum silhouette score**.
* **Tie-breaking in distance matrices** is a standard exam trap — always state your tie-breaking rule.
* **Cophenetic distance** = dendrogram height at which two points are first joined. The **Agglomerative Coefficient (AC)** quantifies how faithfully the dendrogram represents the original distances; AC $\in [0,1]$, higher is better.
* **Single linkage** is sensitive to noise in a *good* way (doesn't force outliers in); **complete linkage** is sensitive to noise in a *bad* way (one outlier can delay a merge).
* **Ward's linkage** strictly requires Euclidean base distance (it uses sum of squares).
* For **high-dimensional sparse data** (e.g., text), prefer **cosine distance** with average or complete linkage.
* **Practical implementation in Python:** `scipy.cluster.hierarchy.linkage`, `scipy.cluster.hierarchy.fcluster`, `scipy.cluster.hierarchy.dendrogram`, plus `sklearn.metrics.silhouette_score` for evaluation.
* **Standard practice:** always `StandardScaler` or `MinMaxScaler` the data before HAC to prevent high-magnitude features from dominating the distance matrix.
* **Big-O memorisation tip for KTU:** $n$ points → proximity matrix of size $n^2$ → $n - 1$ merges → total cost $\mathcal{O}(n^3)$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and Implementation of PCA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAML407 - MACHINE LEARNING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Design and Implementation of PCA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** is a classical, unsupervised, linear **dimensionality reduction** technique used in machine learning and multivariate statistics. Given a high-dimensional dataset $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}$ containing $n$ samples and $p$ original features, PCA mathematically transforms this data into a new coordinate system defined by mutually orthogonal axes called **Principal Components (PCs)**. These components are ordered such that the first principal component (PC1) captures the **maximum possible variance** in the data, the second principal component (PC2) captures the maximum remaining variance orthogonal to PC1, and so on.

Formally, PCA computes an orthogonal linear transformation

$$Y = X W$$

where $W \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times k}$ is a matrix whose columns are the top $k$ eigenvectors of the sample **covariance matrix**, and $Y \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}$ is the reduced-dimension representation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** PCA belongs to the *Unsupervised Learning – Feature Engineering* cluster. In PCAML407 (Machine Learning Lab), the experiment typically asks you to (a) implement PCA from scratch, (b) compare it with `sklearn.decomposition.PCA`, (c) visualize the explained variance, and (d) apply it as a pre-processing step before a downstream classifier (KNN/SVM/Logistic Regression).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – "The Best Camera Angle" 🎥

Imagine you are a cinematographer asked to photograph a 3D statue (your data) using a **2D camera** (your reduced space). You are free to rotate the camera around the statue.

- A **bad angle** (e.g., looking straight down) flattens the statue into a thin line — most of its shape (variance) is lost.
- A **good angle** (e.g., a 3/4 view) preserves the silhouette, the depth, and almost all the recognizable features.

PCA behaves like that ideal cinematographer. It rotates the original axes so that:
1. The first new axis (PC1) points in the direction of the **greatest spread** of the data — the camera angle that captures the most "shape."
2. Each subsequent axis (PC2, PC3, …) is **perpendicular** to all previous axes and captures the next biggest slice of remaining variance.
3. You can then **throw away** the last few axes (low-variance directions) because they contain little information — analogous to dropping the camera's vertical perspective if the statue is flat.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Takeaway:** PCA doesn't "create" new data; it **re-expresses** the existing data along smarter directions and lets you discard the boring, low-variance ones.

## 1.3 Why PCA Matters in Real Systems

| Engineering Domain | Practical Use of PCA |
|---|---|
| Computer Vision (Face Recognition) | Eigenfaces — reduce thousands of pixels to ~50 components |
| Genomics & Bioinformatics | Visualize high-dimensional gene expression data in 2D/3D |
| Finance | Extract principal risk factors from correlated stock returns |
| NLP | Latent Semantic Analysis (truncated SVD, a close cousin of PCA) |
| Anomaly Detection | Anomalies show large reconstruction error in low-rank PCA space |
| Signal Processing | Denoising by keeping top-$k$ components and reconstructing |

The core metric used universally is the **Explained Variance Ratio (EVR)** — the fraction of total variance captured by each component.

$$\text{EVR}_i = \frac{\lambda_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{p} \lambda_j}$$

This ratio is always $\leq 1$ and the sum across all components equals **1**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic — Five Pillars of PCA

PCA, although a single algorithm, is built on five sequential theoretical pillars. Each pillar answers one *why* question.

### Pillar 1 — Standardization (Z-Score Scaling)
Before computing variance, features with larger numerical ranges will dominate. We rescale every feature to **zero mean** and **unit variance**.

$$z_{ij} = \frac{x_{ij} - \mu_j}{\sigma_j}$$

- $\mu_j$ = mean of feature $j$
- $\sigma_j$ = standard deviation of feature $j$

### Pillar 2 — Covariance Matrix Construction
Variance captures spread in *one* dimension; **covariance** captures how two dimensions vary *together*. For standardized data, the covariance matrix simplifies to the correlation matrix.

$$C = \frac{1}{n-1} X_{std}^{\top} X_{std} \quad \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times p}$$

- $C$ is **symmetric**: $C = C^{\top}$
- $C$ is **positive semi-definite**: all eigenvalues $\lambda_i \geq 0$

### Pillar 3 — Eigendecomposition
We solve the characteristic equation

$$C v_i = \lambda_i v_i$$

to obtain $p$ eigenvalue–eigenvector pairs $(\lambda_i, v_i)$.

- $v_i$ = direction of the $i$-th principal axis (a unit vector)
- $\lambda_i$ = variance of the data *along* $v_i$

### Pillar 4 — Component Ranking & Selection
Sort eigenvalues in **descending order** $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2 \geq \cdots \geq \lambda_p$. Retain the top $k$ components such that the cumulative explained variance exceeds a chosen threshold (commonly **0.90, 0.95, or 0.99**).

### Pillar 5 — Projection (Dimensionality Reduction)
Form the projection matrix $W_k = [v_1 \; v_2 \; \cdots \; v_k]$ and compute the reduced dataset:

$$Y = X_{std} \, W_k \quad \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}$$

If reconstruction is required, map back: $\hat{X}_{std} = Y W_k^{\top}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "orthogonal"?** Orthogonality of the eigenvectors (a property of real symmetric matrices) guarantees that each principal component carries **independent, non-redundant** information. This is the mathematical reason PCA can compress data without double-counting features.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Glossary | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Standardized value | $z_{ij} = (x_{ij} - \mu_j)/\sigma_j$ | $\mu_j,\sigma_j$ — feature $j$ mean & std | Required when features have different units |
| 2 | Mean vector | $\mu = \tfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ | $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^{p}$ | Column vector of feature means |
| 3 | Covariance matrix | $C = \tfrac{1}{n-1} (X-\mu)^{\top}(X-\mu)$ | $C \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times p}$ | Symmetric, PSD |
| 4 | Eigendecomposition | $C v_i = \lambda_i v_i$ | $v_i$ eigenvector, $\lambda_i$ eigenvalue | $C = V \Lambda V^{\top}$ |
| 5 | Reconstruction / Projection | $Y = X_{std} W_k$ | $W_k \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times k}$ | $k < p$ reduces dimensionality |
| 6 | Explained Variance Ratio (EVR) | $\text{EVR}_i = \lambda_i / \sum_j \lambda_j$ | $0 \leq \text{EVR}_i \leq 1$ | Sum = 1 across all PCs |
| 7 | Cumulative Explained Variance | $CEV_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{EVR}_i$ | Threshold typically $0.90$ or $0.95$ | Choose smallest $k$ meeting threshold |
| 8 | Singular Value Decomposition (alt.) | $X_{std} = U \Sigma V^{\top}$ | $V$ = principal axes | Numerically more stable than eigendecomp |
| 9 | Reconstruction Error (MSE) | $E = \tfrac{1}{n}\sum_i \Vert x_i - \hat{x}_i \Vert^2$ | $\hat{x}_i$ reconstructed sample | Minimized by top-$k$ PCA |
| 10 | Total Variance Identity | $\sum_j \text{Var}(X_j) = \sum_i \lambda_i$ | Conservation of "spread" | Why EVR sums to 1 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical exam pitfall:** The KTU 2024 scheme marks are deducted if you forget the denominator $(n-1)$ in the covariance formula. The $(n-1)$ term is the **Bessel correction** that makes the sample covariance unbiased.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

PCA is the **workhorse pre-processing step** in modern ML pipelines because of three engineering properties:

1. **Curse-of-Dimensionality Mitigation** — As $p$ grows, data becomes sparse in $\mathbb{R}^p$ (Hughes phenomenon). Reducing $p \to k$ restores statistical reliability of downstream models.
2. **Noise Suppression** — In datasets like sensor arrays or images, low-variance directions often correspond to **noise**. Discarding them improves the **signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)**.
3. **Computational Acceleration** — A classifier trained on $k=20$ components instead of $p=5000$ features trains 100×–1000× faster and generalizes better (lower variance).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Industrial Context:** Companies like Netflix (recommender embeddings), Intel (chip-defect detection), and the Mayo Clinic (gene-expression diagnostics) use PCA or its non-linear kernel variant in production.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Why PCA Maximizes Variance

**Goal:** Find a unit direction $v$ (with $\|v\| = 1$) along which the projected data has the **maximum variance**.

Let the centered data matrix be $X_c \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}$ with rows $(x_i - \mu)^{\top}$. The projection of a single sample onto direction $v$ is the scalar

$$y_i = v^{\top}(x_i - \mu)$$

The variance of these projected scalars is

$$\text{Var}(y) = \frac{1}{n-1} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (v^{\top}(x_i - \mu))^2$$

We can rewrite this in matrix form. Let us expand the squared term first.

$$(v^{\top}(x_i - \mu))^2 = v^{\top}(x_i - \mu)(x_i - \mu)^{\top} v$$

Summing over all $i$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} v^{\top}(x_i - \mu)(x_i - \mu)^{\top} v = v^{\top}\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu)(x_i - \mu)^{\top}\right] v$$

We recognize the bracketed term as $(n-1)$ times the sample covariance matrix $C$.

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} v^{\top}(x_i - \mu)(x_i - \mu)^{\top} v = (n-1)\, v^{\top} C v$$

Substituting back into the variance expression:

$$\text{Var}(y) = \frac{1}{n-1} \cdot (n-1)\, v^{\top} C v = v^{\top} C v$$

Therefore the **optimization problem** becomes:

$$\max_{v} \; v^{\top} C v \quad \text{subject to} \quad v^{\top} v = 1$$

We solve this constrained maximization using the **Lagrange multiplier** method.

$$\mathcal{L}(v, \lambda) = v^{\top} C v - \lambda (v^{\top} v - 1)$$

Differentiate with respect to $v$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial v} = 2 C v - 2 \lambda v = 0$$

This simplifies to the famous **eigenvalue equation**:

$$C v = \lambda v$$

Substituting this back into the variance objective confirms that the *eigenvalue equals the variance along the eigenvector*:

$$\text{Var}(y) = v^{\top} C v = v^{\top} (\lambda v) = \lambda \, v^{\top} v = \lambda$$

**Conclusion:** The direction of maximum variance is the eigenvector corresponding to the **largest** eigenvalue of the covariance matrix. Repeating this on the residual subspace (orthogonal complement) yields PC2, PC3, … .

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — Hand-Computed PCA on a 2-D Toy Dataset

Consider $n = 5$ samples, $p = 2$ features:

$$X = \begin{bmatrix} 2.5 & 2.4 \\ 0.5 & 0.7 \\ 2.2 & 2.9 \\ 1.9 & 2.2 \\ 3.1 & 3.0 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 1 — Compute the Mean Vector

$$\mu_j = \frac{1}{5}\sum_{i=1}^{5} x_{ij}$$

$$\mu_1 = \frac{2.5+0.5+2.2+1.9+3.1}{5} = \frac{10.2}{5} = 2.04$$

$$\mu_2 = \frac{2.4+0.7+2.9+2.2+3.0}{5} = \frac{11.2}{5} = 2.24$$

So the mean vector is

$$\mu = \begin{bmatrix} 2.04 \\ 2.24 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 2 — Center the Data

$$X_c = X - \mathbf{1}\mu^{\top} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.46 & 0.16 \\ -1.54 & -1.54 \\ 0.16 & 0.66 \\ -0.14 & -0.04 \\ 1.06 & 0.76 \end{bmatrix}$$

Quick verification: column sums are $\approx 0$ ✔.

### Step 3 — Compute the Covariance Matrix

$$C = \frac{1}{n-1} X_c^{\top} X_c = \frac{1}{4} X_c^{\top} X_c$$

Computing $X_c^{\top} X_c$ entry by entry:

$$X_c^{\top} X_c = \begin{bmatrix} 0.46^2+(-1.54)^2+0.16^2+(-0.14)^2+1.06^2 & 0.46(0.16)+(-1.54)(-1.54)+0.16(0.66)+(-0.14)(-0.04)+1.06(0.76) \\ \text{(symmetric)} & 0.16^2+(-1.54)^2+0.66^2+(-0.04)^2+0.76^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Numerically:

$$X_c^{\top} X_c = \begin{bmatrix} 4.354 & 4.102 \\ 4.102 & 3.952 \end{bmatrix}$$

Therefore:

$$C = \frac{1}{4}\begin{bmatrix} 4.354 & 4.102 \\ 4.102 & 3.952 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.0885 & 1.0255 \\ 1.0255 & 0.9880 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 4 — Solve the Characteristic Equation $\det(C - \lambda I) = 0$

$$\det \begin{bmatrix} 1.0885 - \lambda & 1.0255 \\ 1.0255 & 0.9880 - \lambda \end{bmatrix} = 0$$

Expanding:

$$(1.0885 - \lambda)(0.9880 - \lambda) - (1.0255)^2 = 0$$

$$1.0756 - 1.0885\lambda - 0.9880\lambda + \lambda^2 - 1.0517 = 0$$

$$\lambda^2 - 2.0765\lambda + 0.0239 = 0$$

Applying the quadratic formula $\lambda = \tfrac{b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$:

$$\lambda = \frac{2.0765 \pm \sqrt{(2.0765)^2 - 4(1)(0.0239)}}{2} = \frac{2.0765 \pm \sqrt{4.3119 - 0.0956}}{2} = \frac{2.0765 \pm \sqrt{4.2163}}{2} = \frac{2.0765 \pm 2.0534}{2}$$

This gives two eigenvalues:

$$\lambda_1 = \frac{2.0765 + 2.0534}{2} = 2.0650 \quad ; \quad \lambda_2 = \frac{2.0765 - 2.0534}{2} = 0.0116$$

**Sanity check:** $\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 = 2.0766 \approx \text{tr}(C) = 1.0885 + 0.9880$ ✔.

### Step 5 — Compute the Eigenvectors

For $\lambda_1 = 2.0650$, solve $(C - \lambda_1 I)v_1 = 0$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 1.0885 - 2.0650 & 1.0255 \\ 1.0255 & 0.9880 - 2.0650 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_{11} \\ v_{12} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.9765 & 1.0255 \\ 1.0255 & -1.0770 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_{11} \\ v_{12} \end{bmatrix} = 0$$

From the first row: $-0.9765\, v_{11} + 1.0255\, v_{12} = 0 \Rightarrow v_{11} = 1.0255/0.9765 \cdot v_{12} \approx 1.0502\, v_{12}$.

Choose $v_{12} = 1$, then $v_{11} \approx 1.0502$. Normalize: $\|v_1\| = \sqrt{1.0502^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{2.1029} \approx 1.4502$.

$$v_1 = \frac{1}{1.4502}\begin{bmatrix} 1.0502 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.7242 \\ 0.6896 \end{bmatrix}$$

For $\lambda_2 = 0.0116$, the eigenvector is **orthogonal** to $v_1$:

$$v_2 = \begin{bmatrix} -0.6896 \\ 0.7242 \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{(rotated 90° in the 2-D plane)}$$

### Step 6 — Explained Variance Ratio

$$\text{Total variance} = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 = 2.0766$$

$$\text{EVR}_1 = \frac{2.0650}{2.0766} = 0.9944 \;\; (99.44\%)$$

$$\text{EVR}_2 = \frac{0.0116}{2.0766} = 0.0056 \;\; (0.56\%)$$

**Conclusion:** PC1 alone explains **99.44 %** of the variance. We can reduce the dataset from 2-D to 1-D with negligible information loss.

### Step 7 — Project Data onto PC1

$$Y = X_c \, v_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.46 & 0.16 \\ -1.54 & -1.54 \\ 0.16 & 0.66 \\ -0.14 & -0.04 \\ 1.06 & 0.76 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.7242 \\ 0.6896 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4435 \\ -2.1770 \\ 0.5708 \\ -0.1290 \\ 1.2917 \end{bmatrix}$$

These five scalars form the 1-D reduced representation of the original 2-D data.

## 3.3 Comparison Matrix — Three Equivalent Routes to PCA

| Method | Core Equation | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|
| Eigendecomposition of $C$ | $C = V\Lambda V^{\top}$ | Conceptually clean, ties to variance | Numerically unstable for large $p$ |
| SVD of $X_c$ | $X_c = U\Sigma V^{\top}$, $V$ = PCs | Numerically stable, works on sparse matrices | Requires care with centering |
| Iterative (Power Method) | $v \leftarrow Cv / \|Cv\|$ | Memory-efficient for huge matrices | Slow convergence, needs many iterations |

All three methods yield the **same principal axes** $V$ up to sign; only the algorithm and numerical stability differ.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Quick Check:** When asked "Prove PCA maximizes variance," the key steps are: (1) write the projected variance as $v^{\top} C v$, (2) introduce the Lagrange constraint $v^{\top} v = 1$, (3) differentiate to obtain $Cv = \lambda v$, (4) conclude that the maximum variance equals the largest eigenvalue.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PCA Workflow — End-to-End Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Raw Dataset X n x p] --> A1[Step 1: Compute Mean Vector mu]
    A1 --> A2[Step 2: Center Data Xc = X minus mu]
    A2 --> A3{Choose Scaling?}
    A3 -->|Yes| A4[Step 3a: Standardize using sigma]
    A3 -->|No| A5[Step 3b: Use raw centered data]
    A4 --> A6[Step 4: Covariance Matrix C]
    A5 --> A6
    A6 --> A7[Step 5: Eigendecomposition Cv = lambda v]
    A7 --> A8[Step 6: Sort Eigenvalues Descending]
    A8 --> A9[Step 7: Choose k via Explained Variance Threshold]
    A9 --> A10[Step 8: Form Projection Matrix Wk from top k eigenvectors]
    A10 --> A11[Step 9: Project Y = Xc Wk]
    A11 --> A12[Output: Reduced Dataset Y n x k]
    A11 --> A13[Optional: Reconstruct Xhat = Y Wk transpose]
    A13 --> A14[Step 10: Compute Reconstruction Error MSE]
    A14 --> A15[Visualize Scree Plot and EVR]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — PCA in a Typical ML Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INGEST[Data Ingestion Layer]
        B1[Raw CSV / Image / Sensor Stream]
    end
    subgraph PREPROC[Pre-Processing Layer]
        B2[Missing Value Imputation]
        B3[Outlier Treatment]
        B4[Z-Score Standardization]
    end
    subgraph PCA_CORE[PCA Engine]
        B5[Covariance Matrix Builder]
        B6[Eigensolver Module]
        B7[Component Ranker]
        B8[Projection Operator]
    end
    subgraph DOWNSTREAM[Downstream ML Tasks]
        B9[KNN Classifier]
        B10[Logistic Regression]
        B11[SVM]
        B12[K-Means Clustering]
        B13[2D/3D Visualization]
    end
    B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B4 --> B5
    B5 --> B6 --> B7 --> B8
    B8 --> B9
    B8 --> B10
    B8 --> B11
    B8 --> B12
    B8 --> B13
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Schematic — Geometric Interpretation of Principal Components

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ORIG[Original 2D Feature Space]
        P1[Sample Points Cloud]
        AX1[Axis X1 horizontal]
        AX2[Axis X2 vertical]
    end
    subgraph ROT[Rotated PC Space]
        P2[Same Cloud Same Shape]
        PC1[PC1 Direction of Max Variance]
        PC2[PC2 Orthogonal to PC1]
    end
    subgraph DROP[Projected 1D Space]
        P3[1D Coordinates along PC1]
    end
    ORIG -->|Rotate by V transpose| ROT
    ROT -->|Discard PC2 axis| DROP
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid for the diagrams:**
> - Diagram 1 (Workflow) traces the **algorithmic** steps in execution order.
> - Diagram 2 (Architecture) shows how PCA fits as a **plug-in module** between pre-processing and the ML model.
> - Diagram 3 (Geometric) clarifies the **rotation + projection** interpretation that KTU examiners love to ask in viva voce.

## 4.4 Scree Plot — Decision Aid for Choosing $k$

A *scree plot* graphs eigenvalue index $i$ (x-axis) versus eigenvalue $\lambda_i$ (y-axis). Look for the **elbow** — the point where the curve flattens. Components to the *left* of the elbow are retained; those to the *right* are dropped.

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Typical Scree Plot Shape"
    x-axis "Principal Component Index" [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8]
    y-axis "Eigenvalue lambda" 0 --> 10
    line [9.5, 3.2, 1.1, 0.4, 0.2, 0.1, 0.05, 0.02]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse the *Kaiser criterion* (retain $\lambda_i > 1$, only valid for standardized data) with the *cumulative variance* rule (retain smallest $k$ such that $CEV_k \geq 0.95$). Mention which rule you are using in your answer.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 2-Mark / 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, May 2024]**

**Q1. Define Principal Component Analysis. State any two limitations of PCA.**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
PCA is an unsupervised linear dimensionality reduction technique that projects data onto orthogonal axes (principal components) ordered by decreasing variance, computed as the eigenvectors of the sample covariance matrix.
**Limitations:**
1. PCA is **linear** — it cannot capture complex non-linear manifolds (use Kernel PCA, t-SNE, or autoencoders instead).
2. PCA assumes that **directions of high variance = directions of high information**, which fails when class separation lies in low-variance directions (e.g., the "needle in a haystack" anomaly case).
3. Principal components are **linear combinations of *all* original features**, making them hard to interpret in domains like medicine or finance.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Why is feature standardization essential before applying PCA? What happens if we skip it?**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Standardization rescales every feature to zero mean and unit variance: $z_{ij} = (x_{ij} - \mu_j)/\sigma_j$.
**Why it is essential:**
- PCA uses variance to rank components. A feature measured in *rupees* (range 0–1,000,000) will dominate a feature measured in *percentage* (range 0–1) simply because of units, not information.
- Standardization puts all features on the **same scale**, ensuring the covariance matrix reflects **true** correlations rather than unit-induced spread.
**If we skip it:** PC1 will simply be the original feature with the largest scale, regardless of its actual predictive value. The "principal components" will be uninformative and biased.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper, Module 2]**

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the step-by-step procedure of Principal Component Analysis. State the role of the covariance matrix and the eigenvalue equation in PCA. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Consider the dataset $X = \{ (1,2), (3,3), (5,5), (7,8) \}$ with 4 samples and 2 features. Compute the principal components using PCA. Find the explained variance ratio of PC1. State whether 1-D projection is sufficient at a 95% threshold. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Procedure (Block Diagram already shown in Section 4.1):**

1. **Standardize** the data matrix $X$ column-wise using the formula $z = (x - \mu)/\sigma$.
2. **Compute the covariance matrix** $C = \frac{1}{n-1} X_c^{\top} X_c$ (or correlation matrix if standardized).
3. **Solve the eigenvalue problem** $C v = \lambda v$ to obtain eigenvector–eigenvalue pairs.
4. **Rank** eigenvalues in descending order $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2 \geq \cdots \geq \lambda_p$.
5. **Choose $k$** such that cumulative explained variance $\geq$ threshold (e.g., 0.95).
6. **Construct projection matrix** $W_k = [v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_k]$.
7. **Project**: $Y = X_{std} W_k$.

**Role of the covariance matrix:** Encodes both the variance of each feature (diagonal) and the pairwise covariance between features (off-diagonal). Eigenvectors of $C$ are the directions in which the data varies the most.

**Role of the eigenvalue equation:** $Cv = \lambda v$ — eigenvectors $v$ give the *direction* (axis) of each principal component and eigenvalues $\lambda$ give the *magnitude* of variance along that direction.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Step 1 — Data Matrix:**

$$X = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 3 \\ 5 & 5 \\ 7 & 8 \end{bmatrix}, \quad n = 4,\; p = 2$$

**Step 2 — Mean Vector:**

$$\mu_1 = \frac{1+3+5+7}{4} = 4, \quad \mu_2 = \frac{2+3+5+8}{4} = 4.5$$

$$\mu = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 4.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Centered Data:**

$$X_c = \begin{bmatrix} -3 & -2.5 \\ -1 & -1.5 \\ 1 & 0.5 \\ 3 & 3.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Covariance Matrix:**

$$X_c^{\top} X_c = \begin{bmatrix} (-3)^2+(-1)^2+1^2+3^2 & (-3)(-2.5)+(-1)(-1.5)+(1)(0.5)+(3)(3.5) \\ \text{sym} & (-2.5)^2+(-1.5)^2+0.5^2+3.5^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Computing each entry:

- $(1,1)$: $9+1+1+9 = 20$
- $(1,2)$: $7.5+1.5+0.5+10.5 = 20$
- $(2,2)$: $6.25+2.25+0.25+12.25 = 21$

$$X_c^{\top} X_c = \begin{bmatrix} 20 & 20 \\ 20 & 21 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$C = \frac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 20 & 20 \\ 20 & 21 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 6.6667 & 6.6667 \\ 6.6667 & 7.0000 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Eigenvalues:**

$$\det(C - \lambda I) = (6.6667 - \lambda)(7 - \lambda) - 6.6667^2 = 0$$

$$\lambda^2 - 13.6667\lambda + 46.6667 - 44.4444 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda^2 - 13.6667\lambda + 2.2222 = 0$$

$$\lambda = \frac{13.6667 \pm \sqrt{186.778 - 8.889}}{2} = \frac{13.6667 \pm \sqrt{177.889}}{2} = \frac{13.6667 \pm 13.337}{2}$$

$$\lambda_1 = \frac{13.6667 + 13.337}{2} = 13.502, \quad \lambda_2 = \frac{13.6667 - 13.337}{2} = 0.165$$

**Step 6 — Explained Variance Ratio:**

$$\text{Total} = 13.502 + 0.165 = 13.667$$

$$\text{EVR}_1 = \frac{13.502}{13.667} = 0.9879 \quad (98.79\%)$$

$$\text{EVR}_2 = \frac{0.165}{13.667} = 0.0121 \quad (1.21\%)$$

**Step 7 — Decision on $k$:**

Since $\text{EVR}_1 = 98.79\% > 95\%$, **PC1 alone is sufficient** to capture 95% of the variance. We may safely project to $k = 1$ dimension.

**Step 8 — PC1 Direction (Optional):**

For $\lambda_1$: $(6.6667 - 13.502)v_{11} + 6.6667 v_{12} = 0 \Rightarrow -6.8353 v_{11} + 6.6667 v_{12} = 0$

$\Rightarrow v_{11}/v_{12} = 6.6667/6.8353 \approx 0.9753$. After normalization, $v_1 \approx [0.6987,\; 0.7154]^{\top}$.

**Final Answer:** PC1 captures **98.79 %** of the variance; **1-D projection is sufficient** at the 95 % threshold.

---

### 📗 Question B — Alternative Choice (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the **geometric and statistical interpretation** of principal components. How are eigenvectors of the covariance matrix related to the directions of maximum variance? *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Implement PCA from scratch in Python on the `Iris` dataset (without using `sklearn.decomposition.PCA`). Plot (i) the scree plot, (ii) the cumulative explained variance curve, and (iii) a 2-D scatter of the first two principal components colored by class label. Compare your implementation's explained variance ratios with those of `sklearn.decomposition.PCA`. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Geometric interpretation:**
- The principal components are the **orthogonal axes** of an ellipsoid that best fits the data cloud.
- The **first principal component (PC1)** is the longest axis of this ellipsoid — the direction along which the data is maximally stretched.
- The **subsequent components (PC2, PC3, ...)** are the successively shorter, mutually perpendicular axes.
- The square root of each eigenvalue $\sqrt{\lambda_i}$ is proportional to the **semi-axis length** of the ellipsoid along PC $i$.

**Statistical interpretation:**
- Eigenvectors are the **principal axes** of the multivariate Gaussian distribution that best models the data.
- Eigenvalues are the **variances** of the data along these axes (assuming zero mean).
- The cumulative explained variance is the **fraction of total variance** retained in a low-dimensional projection.

**Mathematical link:** Maximizing $v^{\top} C v$ subject to $v^{\top} v = 1$ via Lagrange multipliers yields $Cv = \lambda v$. The eigenvector $v$ of the largest eigenvalue points along the **direction of maximum variance** (proven in Section 3.1 of these notes).

#### Model Solution — (b) — Reference Python Implementation

```python
# =========================================================
# PCA FROM SCRATCH vs SKLEARN — Reference Implementation
# Course: PCAML407  |  KTU 2024 Scheme
# =========================================================
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA as SKPCA

# ---- 1. Load and standardize ----
iris   = load_iris()
X      = iris.data                   # shape (150, 4)
y      = iris.target                 # class labels 0,1,2
X_std  = StandardScaler().fit_transform(X)

# ---- 2. Manual PCA via eigendecomposition ----
n      = X_std.shape[0]
C      = (X_std.T @ X_std) / (n - 1)         # covariance matrix
eigval, eigvec = np.linalg.eigh(C)           # ascending order
order   = np.argsort(eigval)[::-1]           # descending
eigval  = eigval[order]
eigvec  = eigvec[:, order]

# Explained Variance Ratio
evr_manual = eigval / eigval.sum()
print("Manual EVR :", np.round(evr_manual, 4))

# ---- 3. sklearn PCA for comparison ----
sk    = SKPCA().fit(X_std)
print("Sklearn EVR:", np.round(sk.explained_variance_ratio_, 4))

# ---- 4. Project onto first two PCs ----
Y_manual = X_std @ eigvec[:, :2]
Y_sk     = sk.transform(X_std)[:, :2]

# ---- 5. Plots ----
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(18, 5))

# (i) Scree plot
axes[0].plot(range(1, 5), eigval, 'o-', color='steelblue')
axes[0].set_title("Scree Plot")
axes[0].set_xlabel("Principal Component")
axes[0].set_ylabel("Eigenvalue (Variance)")
axes[0].grid(alpha=0.3)

# (ii) Cumulative explained variance
axes[1].plot(range(1, 5), np.cumsum(evr_manual), 'o-', color='darkorange')
axes[1].axhline(0.95, color='red', ls='--', label='95% threshold')
axes[1].set_title("Cumulative Explained Variance")
axes[1].set_xlabel("Number of Components")
axes[1].set_ylabel("Cumulative EVR")
axes[1].legend(); axes[1].grid(alpha=0.3)

# (iii) 2D scatter
for cls in np.unique(y):
    axes[2].scatter(Y_manual[y == cls, 0], Y_manual[y == cls, 1],
                    label=iris.target_names[cls], s=40, alpha=0.8)
axes[2].set_title("Iris in PC1-PC2 Space (Manual PCA)")
axes[2].set_xlabel("PC1"); axes[2].set_ylabel("PC2")
axes[2].legend(); axes[2].grid(alpha=0.3)

plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

**Expected Output Snapshot:**

| Component | Manual EVR | Sklearn EVR |
|---|---|---|
| PC1 | 0.7296 | 0.7296 |
| PC2 | 0.2285 | 0.2285 |
| PC3 | 0.0367 | 0.0367 |
| PC4 | 0.0052 | 0.0052 |

The two implementations **agree to 4 decimal places** ✔. The first two components explain **95.81 %** of the variance, justifying a 2-D visualization.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to standardize** before computing the covariance matrix — costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up eigenvalues with explained variance** — eigenvalues are raw variances, EVR is normalized.
> 3. **Reporting PC sign inconsistently** — sign of an eigenvector is arbitrary, but you must use the *same* sign in projection and reconstruction. KTU evaluators flag this.
> 4. **Not stating the threshold** when choosing $k$ — always say "k is chosen such that cumulative EVR ≥ 0.95".
> 5. **Hand-calculation rounding errors** in 7-mark problems — keep at least 4 decimals throughout.
> 6. **Forgetting the Bessel correction $(n-1)$** in the covariance formula.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

- **PCA = linear, unsupervised, variance-maximizing dimensionality reduction.**
- **The five-step pipeline:** Standardize → Covariance → Eigendecomposition → Rank → Project.
- **Eigenvectors of $C$ are the principal axes; eigenvalues are the variances along them.**
- **Always standardize features** when they have different units or scales.
- **Cumulative Explained Variance (CEV)** is the principal tool for choosing $k$ — typical threshold is **0.90, 0.95, or 0.99**.
- **EVR$_i = \lambda_i / \sum_j \lambda_j$**; the sum over all PCs equals 1.
- **Scree plot** shows $\lambda_i$ vs. component index; look for the *elbow*.
- **Kaiser rule** (keep $\lambda_i > 1$) is valid only after standardization.
- **Reconstruction error** $= \frac{1}{n}\sum_i \|x_i - \hat{x}_i\|^2$ is minimized by retaining the top-$k$ PCs.
- **Sign of an eigenvector is arbitrary** — flip carefully and document your choice.
- **PCA fails on non-linear manifolds** — use **Kernel PCA**, **t-SNE**, **UMAP**, or **autoencoders** instead.
- **Variants worth mentioning in viva:** Incremental PCA (for streaming data), Sparse PCA (for interpretability), Kernel PCA (for non-linearity), Randomized PCA (for large $p$).
- **The $(n-1)$ divisor** in the covariance formula is the Bessel correction — KTU examiners love to test this.
- **Eigendecomposition vs SVD:** SVD is numerically more stable and is the default in `sklearn.decomposition.PCA`.
- **PCA assumes zero mean** — always center the data, even if you don't standardize.
- **Dimensionality choice in practice:** use the smallest $k$ such that $CEV_k \geq$ threshold; report the threshold explicitly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and Implementation of Expectation Maximization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAML407 - MACHINE LEARNING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Design and Implementation of Expectation Maximization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Expectation–Maximization (EM) Algorithm:** A general-purpose iterative optimization method used in Machine Learning to find the **Maximum Likelihood Estimates (MLE)** or **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP)** parameters of statistical models when the data is *incomplete*, *missing*, or contains *latent (hidden) variables*. The algorithm alternates between two steps — the **Expectation (E) Step**, which computes the expected value of the latent variables given current parameter estimates, and the **Maximization (M) Step**, which re-estimates the parameters by maximizing the expected log-likelihood computed in the E-step.

The EM algorithm was formally published by **Arthur P. Dempster, Nan M. Laird, and Donald B. Rubin** in their landmark 1977 paper *"Maximum Likelihood from Incomplete Data via the EM Algorithm"* (Journal of the Royal Statistical Society, Series B). It is a foundational technique listed under the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **unsupervised learning** and **probabilistic graphical models**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Two-Coin Experiment

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "Guessing the Bias of Two Biased Coins"**
> Imagine you have **two biased coins**, Coin A and Coin B. You randomly pick one coin (with equal probability) and toss it **10 times**, recording only the number of heads — but **you are NOT told which coin you used for each experiment**. The hidden information is *which coin* was used. You must still estimate the bias ($\theta_A$, $\theta_B$) of each coin.
>
> - The **E-Step** acts like a "soft assignment" — for each experiment, you compute the *probability* it came from Coin A vs Coin B, given current bias estimates.
> - The **M-Step** acts like "re-weighing" — you update the bias of each coin using these soft assignments as fractional counts.
>
> Repeat until the bias estimates stop changing. This iterative refinement is the heart of EM.

## 1.3 Why EM is Needed in ML

In many ML problems, the log-likelihood function cannot be maximized directly in closed form because the latent variables make the likelihood intractable. EM provides a **monotonically non-decreasing** guarantee:

$$\ell(\theta^{(t+1)}) \geq \ell(\theta^{(t)})$$

> [!TIP]
> **Standard EM Metrics to Remember:**
> - **Convergence Criterion:** $\vert \ell(\theta^{(t+1)}) - \ell(\theta^{(t)}) \vert \leq 10^{-6}$
> - **Guarantee:** Convergence to a *local* (not necessarily global) optimum.
> - **Sensitivity:** Highly dependent on initial parameter values — multiple random restarts are recommended.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Step Operational Logic

The EM algorithm is decomposed into the following structured phases:

### **E-Step (Expectation Step)**

- **Goal:** Compute the posterior distribution of the latent variables $Z$ given the observed data $X$ and the current parameter estimate $\theta^{(t)}$.
- **Mathematical Action:** Evaluate the expected value of the complete-data log-likelihood $Q(\theta \mid \theta^{(t)})$.
- **Output:** A "responsibility" matrix $\gamma(z_{nk})$ representing the probability that observation $n$ belongs to latent component $k$.

### **M-Step (Maximization Step)**

- **Goal:** Update the parameters $\theta$ by maximizing the expected log-likelihood $Q$ computed in the E-step.
- **Mathematical Action:** Solve $\theta^{(t+1)} = \arg\max_{\theta} Q(\theta \mid \theta^{(t)})$.
- **Output:** New parameter estimates that will be fed back into the next E-step.

### **Convergence Check**

- **Goal:** Verify that the change in log-likelihood (or parameters) is below a predefined tolerance $\epsilon$.
- **Action:** If $\vert \theta^{(t+1)} - \theta^{(t)} \vert < \epsilon$, terminate; otherwise, return to E-step.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind EM:** Direct maximization of the marginal log-likelihood $\log P(X \mid \theta) = \log \sum_{Z} P(X, Z \mid \theta)$ is intractable because the summation over all latent configurations grows exponentially. EM cleverly introduces a distribution $q(Z)$ over the latent variables and applies **Jensen's Inequality** to construct a tractable lower bound (the **ELBO** — Evidence Lower Bound) that is iteratively tightened.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol / Term** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|
| Observed Data | $X = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N\}$ | Visible / measured variables |
| Latent Variables | $Z = \{z_1, z_2, \ldots, z_N\}$ | Hidden / unobserved variables |
| Complete Log-Likelihood | $\ell_c(\theta) = \log P(X, Z \mid \theta)$ | Joint log-likelihood of $X$ and $Z$ |
| Marginal Log-Likelihood | $\ell(\theta) = \log \sum_{Z} P(X, Z \mid \theta)$ | Observed-data log-likelihood |
| E-Step Function | $Q(\theta \mid \theta^{(t)}) = \mathbb{E}_{Z \mid X, \theta^{(t)}}[\log P(X, Z \mid \theta)]$ | Expected complete log-likelihood |
| Responsibility (GMM) | $\gamma(z_{nk}) = \dfrac{\pi_k \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} \pi_j \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_j, \Sigma_j)}$ | Posterior probability of component $k$ |
| M-Step Mean (GMM) | $\mu_k^{(t+1)} = \dfrac{\sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk}) x_n}{\sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk})}$ | Updated mean for component $k$ |
| M-Step Mixing Coeff. | $\pi_k^{(t+1)} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk})$ | Updated mixture weight |
| M-Step Covariance (GMM) | $\Sigma_k^{(t+1)} = \dfrac{\sum_{n} \gamma(z_{nk}) (x_n - \mu_k)(x_n - \mu_k)^T}{\sum_{n} \gamma(z_{nk})}$ | Updated covariance for component $k$ |
| ELBO | $\mathcal{L}(q, \theta) = \sum_{Z} q(Z) \log \dfrac{P(X, Z \mid \theta)}{q(Z)}$ | Evidence Lower Bound (variational view) |
| KL Divergence | $D_{KL}(q \Vert P) = \sum_{Z} q(Z) \log \dfrac{q(Z)}{P(Z \mid X, \theta)}$ | Gap between ELBO and true log-likelihood |
| Tolerance | $\vert \ell^{(t+1)} - \ell^{(t)} \vert \leq \epsilon$ | Convergence threshold |
| Convergence Guarantee | $\ell(\theta^{(t+1)}) \geq \ell(\theta^{(t)})$ | Monotonic non-decrease of log-likelihood |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bioinformatics:** Gene expression clustering, haplotype inference, multiple sequence alignment.
- **Computer Vision:** Image segmentation, facial recognition with hidden facial landmarks.
- **Natural Language Processing:** Topic modeling via **Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA)**.
- **Speech Processing:** Hidden Markov Model (HMM) parameter estimation via the **Baum–Welch algorithm** (a direct EM application).
- **Medical Imaging:** Mixture model fitting for tumor classification in MRI scans.
- **Anomaly Detection:** Estimating parameters of normal-behavior distributions for outlier flagging.
